headline: LSU's Resilience Tested Against Michigan's Wounded Defense
subhead: Two ranked teams with opposite trajectories collide in Ann Arbor with playoff implications already in view.

xFactor.why: Michigan surrendered 51 points to UCLA and must shore up a secondary that has been exposed early in the season.

keyMatchup: LSU's ability to execute in the red zone against a defensive unit still finding its identity will likely determine field position and momentum swings.
body: LSU arrives undefeated but not unblemished. The Tigers needed a last-possession heroics to escape Oregon, exposing a tendency to play tighter than their ranking suggests. That game tape—where Oregon methodically moved the ball late—should concern Michigan's offense. But the more pressing concern for the home team is what transpired against UCLA: 51 points allowed, a defensive back line that looked overmatched, and questions about whether this unit has the coverage discipline to contain an LSU passing game that only appears sharper with each outing.

Michigan cannot afford to chase the score in this spot. The Wolverines' only loss came against a team they shouldn't have lost to, and the UCLA win, while impressive on the scoreboard, masked structural problems. LSU has shown the kind of close-game poise that wins in November. If Michigan's defense doesn't tighten measurably from last week, the Tigers' experienced signal-caller will carve them up in the second half, and Ann Arbor's playoff hopes take a significant hit by Labor Day.
